Operation 'DeleteSpamReports' has semantic quality issues: missing_output_details, missing_side_effects. Description lacks clarity on what the operation returns and explicit side effects. Added structured input options for better AI agent understanding, clarified the suppression removal side effect, and specified the response behavior. This helps AI agents understand the consequences of this destructive operation.

data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to delete your spam reports.**

Deleting a spam report will remove the suppression, meaning email will once again be sent to the previously suppressed address. This should be avoided unless a recipient indicates they wish to receive email from you again. You can use our [bypass filters](https://sendgrid.com/docs/ui/sending-email/index-suppressions/#bypass-suppressions) to deliver messages to otherwise suppressed addresses when exceptions are required.

There are two options for deleting spam reports: 

1. You can delete all spam reports by setting the `delete_all` field to `true` in the request body.
2. You can delete a list of select spam reports by specifying the email addresses in the `emails` array of the request body.
data.description_suggestion:
**This endpoint allows you to delete spam report suppressions.**

Deleting a spam report removes the suppression, allowing email to be sent again to the previously suppressed address. This action should be avoided unless a recipient explicitly requests to receive email from you again.

**Input Options:**
1. Delete all spam reports by setting `delete_all` to `true` in the request body
2. Delete specific spam reports by providing email addresses in the `emails` array

**Side Effects:** Removes suppression status for specified addresses, resuming email delivery to those recipients. Returns a success response with no body content on successful deletion.
data.reasoning: Description lacks clarity on what the operation returns and explicit side effects. Added structured input options for better AI agent understanding, clarified the suppression removal side effect, and specified the response behavior. This helps AI agents understand the consequences of this destructive operation.

data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to delete a specific spam report by email address.**

Deleting a spam report will remove the suppression, meaning email will once again be sent to the previously suppressed address. This should be avoided unless a recipient indicates they wish to receive email from you again. You can use our [bypass filters](https://sendgrid.com/docs/ui/sending-email/index-suppressions/#bypass-suppressions) to deliver messages to otherwise suppressed addresses when exceptions are required.
data.description_suggestion:
**This endpoint allows you to delete a specific spam report suppression by email address.**

Provide the email address as a path parameter to remove the spam report suppression for that address. Deleting a spam report removes the suppression, allowing email to be sent again to the previously suppressed address.

**Side Effects:** Removes suppression status for the specified address, resuming email delivery to that recipient. This action should be avoided unless the recipient explicitly requests to receive email from you again. Returns a success response with no body content on successful deletion.
data.reasoning: Description lacks clarity on the email parameter requirement and explicit response/side effects. Added parameter context and clarified the suppression removal side effect and response behavior for better AI agent understanding of this destructive operation.

message: Operation 'ListGlobalSuppression' has semantic quality issues: inconsistent_terminology. Description uses 'globally suppressed' but lacks context that these are unsubscribes from all emails. Per API context, global suppressions represent addresses unsubscribed from all messages. Added clarification and terminology consistency (unsubscribed/suppressions) to help AI agents understand the distinction between global and group-based suppressions. Also fixed minor grammar issue ('email address' should be 'email addresses').

data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to retrieve a paginated list of all email address that are globally suppressed.**

You can use the `limit` query parameter to set the page size. If your list contains more items than the page size permits, you can make multiple requests. Use the `offset` query parameter to control the position in the list from which to start retrieving additional items.
data.description_suggestion:
**This endpoint allows you to retrieve a paginated list of all email addresses that are globally suppressed (unsubscribed).**

Global suppressions represent addresses that have unsubscribed from all of your emails. You can use the `limit` query parameter to set the page size. If your list contains more items than the page size permits, you can make multiple requests. Use the `offset` query parameter to control the position in the list from which to start retrieving additional items.
data.reasoning: Description uses 'globally suppressed' but lacks context that these are unsubscribes from all emails. Per API context, global suppressions represent addresses unsubscribed from all messages. Added clarification and terminology consistency (unsubscribed/suppressions) to help AI agents understand the distinction between global and group-based suppressions. Also fixed minor grammar issue ('email address' should be 'email addresses').

message: Operation 'DeleteSuppressionBounces' has semantic quality issues: vague_summary, missing_output_details. Original summary 'Delete bounces' is too generic and doesn't indicate the dual-mode functionality. Description lacks clarity about what constitutes a successful response and doesn't explicitly state the mutual exclusivity constraint upfront. Enhanced summary specifies the operation supports both bulk and selective deletion. Enhanced description clarifies the mutually exclusive parameter constraint, adds return value information, and improves AI agent understanding of when to use each deletion mode.

data.current_description:
This endpoint allows you to delete all emails on your bounces list.

There are two options for deleting bounced emails: 

1. You can delete all bounced emails by setting `delete_all` to `true` in the request body. 
2. You can delete a selection of bounced emails by specifying the email addresses in the `emails` array of the request body. 

**WARNING:** You can not have both `emails` and `delete_all` set.
data.summary_suggestion: Delete bounced emails by selection or bulk
data.description_suggestion: Removes email addresses from your bounce suppression list. Supports two deletion modes: bulk deletion of all bounced emails by setting `delete_all` to `true`, or selective deletion by specifying email addresses in the `emails` array. Note: `emails` and `delete_all` parameters are mutually exclusive - only one can be set per request. Returns success confirmation upon completion.
data.reasoning: Original summary 'Delete bounces' is too generic and doesn't indicate the dual-mode functionality. Description lacks clarity about what constitutes a successful response and doesn't explicitly state the mutual exclusivity constraint upfront. Enhanced summary specifies the operation supports both bulk and selective deletion. Enhanced description clarifies the mutually exclusive parameter constraint, adds return value information, and improves AI agent understanding of when to use each deletion mode.

data.current_description: This endpoint will return the total number of bounces by classification in descending order for each day. You can retrieve the bounce classification totals in CSV format by specifying `"text/csv"` in the Accept header.
data.summary_suggestion: Retrieve bounce totals by classification and day
data.description_suggestion: Retrieves aggregated bounce statistics grouped by bounce classification type, with daily breakdowns in descending order. Supports multiple response formats: JSON (default) or CSV format by specifying `"text/csv"` in the Accept header. Returns bounce counts for each classification category per day, enabling analysis of bounce patterns over time. Useful for understanding which bounce types are most prevalent in your suppression data.
data.reasoning: Original summary lacks specificity about the temporal dimension (daily breakdown). Description doesn't clearly explain the output structure or use cases. Enhanced summary specifies both classification grouping and daily time series. Enhanced description clarifies the response format options, output structure, and provides context about the operation's analytical purpose for AI agents to better understand when to use it.

data.current_summary: Search for suppressions within a group
data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to search a suppression group for multiple suppressions.**

When given a list of email addresses and a group ID, this endpoint will only return the email addresses that have been unsubscribed from the given group.
data.description_suggestion:
**This endpoint allows you to search a suppression group to identify which email addresses have been unsubscribed from that group.**

Provide a list of email addresses and a group ID. The endpoint returns only those email addresses from your list that are currently suppressed (unsubscribed) from the specified suppression group. This is useful for checking suppression status before sending to a group or validating recipient lists against group-specific suppressions.
data.reasoning: Description lacks clarity about the expected output format and practical use cases. Added explicit explanation of what is returned (filtered list of suppressed addresses), clarified the filtering behavior, and included context about when an AI agent should use this operation (pre-send validation, recipient list verification). This improves AI agent interpretability for determining when this operation is appropriate.

data.current_description: **This endpoint allows you to retrieve a list of all suppressions.**
data.description_suggestion:
**This endpoint allows you to retrieve a paginated list of all suppressed email addresses across all suppression groups.**

Returns all email addresses that have been unsubscribed from any suppression group in your account. This includes addresses suppressed due to bounces, blocks, invalid emails, spam reports, and explicit unsubscribes. Use pagination parameters to control the result set size and navigate through large suppression lists.

data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to delete all email addresses on your blocks list.**

There are two options for deleting blocked emails: 

1. You can delete all blocked emails by setting `delete_all` to `true` in the request body. 
2. You can delete a selection of blocked emails by specifying the email addresses in the `emails` array of the request body.
data.summary_suggestion: Delete blocked email addresses from blocks list
data.description_suggestion:
**This endpoint allows you to delete email addresses from your blocks list.**

There are two options for deleting blocked emails:

1. Delete all blocked emails by setting `delete_all` to `true` in the request body.
2. Delete specific blocked emails by providing an array of email addresses in the `emails` field.

Deleting a blocked address will allow emails to be sent to that address again. Use this operation when a previously blocked address should be reconsidered for sending.

data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to create a new suppression group.**

To add an email address to the suppression group, [create a Suppression](https://docs.sendgrid.com/api-reference/suppressions-suppressions/add-suppressions-to-a-suppression-group).
data.description_suggestion: Creates a new suppression group (also called an unsubscribe group or ASM group) that allows recipients to unsubscribe from specific categories of emails rather than all messages. Requires authentication and a request body with group name and description. Returns the newly created suppression group object with its assigned group ID. Use the returned group ID to add email addresses to this group or manage its suppressions.

data.current_summary: Retrieve all suppression groups associated with the user.
data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to retrieve a list of all suppression groups created by this user.**

This endpoint can also return information for multiple group IDs that you include in your request. To add a group ID to your request, simply append `?id=123456&id=123456`, with the appropriate group IDs.
data.description_suggestion: Retrieves a list of all suppression groups (unsubscribe groups) created by the authenticated user. Supports filtering by specific group IDs using query parameters (e.g., ?id=123456&id=789012). Returns an array of suppression group objects, each containing group ID, name, description, and suppression count. Useful for discovering available groups or checking specific group configurations.

data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to delete a suppression group.**

If a recipient uses the "one-click unsubscribe" option on an email associated with a deleted group, that recipient will be added to the global suppression list.

Deleting a suppression group will remove the suppression, meaning email will once again be sent to the previously suppressed addresses. This should be avoided unless a recipient indicates they wish to receive email from you again. You can use our [bypass filters](https://sendgrid.com/docs/ui/sending-email/index-suppressions/#bypass-suppressions) to deliver messages to otherwise suppressed addresses when exceptions are required.
data.description_suggestion: Deletes a suppression group (unsubscribe group) by its group ID. Requires authentication and a valid group_id path parameter. Important side effects: (1) Addresses previously suppressed by this group will no longer be suppressed and will receive emails again; (2) If recipients use one-click unsubscribe on emails associated with this deleted group, they will be added to the global suppression list instead. Use bypass filters if you need to send to previously suppressed addresses. Returns HTTP 204 No Content on successful deletion.
data.reasoning: Summary has inconsistent capitalization. Description is verbose but lacks clarity on return value and has important side effects scattered throughout. Reorganized to clearly state the action, parameters, side effects, and return value. Added HTTP response code for completeness and AI agent clarity.

data.current_summary: Add suppressions to a suppression group
data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to add email addresses to an unsubscribe group.**

If you attempt to add suppressions to a group that has been deleted or does not exist, the suppressions will be added to the global suppressions list.
data.summary_suggestion: Add email addresses to a suppression group
data.description_suggestion: Adds one or more email addresses to a suppression group (unsubscribe group), marking them as suppressed for that group. Requires authentication, a valid group_id path parameter, and a request body containing an array of email addresses. Returns HTTP 201 Created on success. Important: If the specified group has been deleted or does not exist, the email addresses will be added to the global suppression list instead. Useful for bulk suppressing addresses or programmatically managing group suppressions.
data.reasoning: Summary uses 'suppressions' (noun) instead of 'email addresses' which is less clear for AI agents. data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to retrieve a global suppression. You can also use this endpoint to confirm if an email address is already globally suppresed.**

If the email address you include in the URL path parameter `{email}` is already globally suppressed, the response will include that email address. If the address you enter for `{email}` is not globally suppressed, an empty JSON object `{}` will be returned.
data.summary_suggestion: Check if email address is globally suppressed
data.description_suggestion: Retrieves the global suppression status for a specific email address. Returns the email address object if it is globally suppressed, or an empty JSON object `{}` if the address is not suppressed. Use this endpoint to verify whether an email address has been globally suppressed from all SendGrid messages.
data.reasoning: Original summary 'Retrieve a Global Suppression' is vague and doesn't clarify the dual purpose (checking suppression status). The description, while containing relevant details, buries the key information about the two possible response states. Improved summary emphasizes the verification aspect. Improved description clarifies the conditional response behavior and explains the practical use case for AI agents.

data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to remove an email address from the global suppressions group.**

Deleting a suppression group will remove the suppression, meaning email will once again be sent to the previously suppressed addresses. This should be avoided unless a recipient indicates they wish to receive email from you again. You can use our [bypass filters](https://sendgrid.com/docs/ui/sending-email/index-suppressions/#bypass-suppressions) to deliver messages to otherwise suppressed addresses when exceptions are required.
data.description_suggestion: Removes an email address from the global suppressions group, allowing emails to be sent to that address again. **Side effects:** Once removed from global suppressions, the address will receive emails from all SendGrid sends unless suppressed through other mechanisms (e.g., unsubscribe groups, blocks, bounces). This action should only be performed if the recipient has explicitly requested to receive emails again. For temporary exceptions, consider using bypass filters instead of removing the suppression.

data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to retrieve a paginated list of all invalid email addresses.**

You can use the `limit` query parameter to set the page size. If your list contains more items than the page size permits, you can make multiple requests. Use the `offset` query parameter to control the position in the list from which to start retrieving additional items.
data.summary_suggestion: List invalid email addresses with pagination
data.description_suggestion: Retrieves a paginated list of all email addresses that have been marked as invalid by SendGrid. Invalid emails are addresses that failed delivery due to format errors or other validation issues. Use the `limit` parameter to set page size (default behavior) and `offset` parameter to navigate through pages. Returns an array of InvalidEmail objects containing the email address and timestamp information.
data.reasoning: Summary 'Retrieve all invalid emails' is vague and doesn't indicate pagination capability. Description lacks context about what 'invalid emails' means in the SendGrid domain (format errors, validation failures). Missing information about response structure. Improved summary includes pagination context. Improved description explains the domain meaning of 'invalid emails', clarifies pagination parameters, and specifies the response format for better AI agent understanding.

data.current_description:
**This endpoint allows you to remove email addresses from your invalid email address list.**

There are two options for deleting invalid email addresses: 

1) You can delete all invalid email addresses by setting `delete_all` to true in the request body.
2) You can delete some invalid email addresses by specifying certain addresses in an array in the request body.
data.description_suggestion: Removes one or more email addresses from the invalid email address list. Supports two deletion modes: (1) Delete all invalid emails by setting `delete_all: true` in the request body, or (2) Delete specific emails by providing an array of email addresses. **Side effects:** Removed addresses will no longer be suppressed as invalid and may be included in future sends. Returns a success response (204 No Content) on successful deletion. Note: This operation does not affect suppressions in other categories (blocks, bounces, spam reports).
data.reasoning: Summary is adequate but could be more specific. Description explains the two deletion modes clearly but lacks information about response format and side effects. Missing context about what happens after deletion and how this relates to other suppression types. Improved description adds response format, explicit side effects, and clarifies the scope of this operation relative to other suppression categories.
